title: — SSO Token Verification (auth-service)
content:
File: services/auth-service/src/index.js

The /auth/sso/verify-token endpoint calls jwt.decode(id_token) instead of
jwt.verify(). A forged token is blindly trusted.

Implementation:
- Fetch the Identity Provider's public key from /.well-known/jwks.json
- Cache the JWKS response with a 1-hour TTL in Redis
- Replace jwt.decode with jwt.verify(id_token, publicKey, { algorithms: ['RS256'] })
- After verification validate iss, aud, and exp claims explicitly
- Return 401 with a generic message on any verification failure
- Log the failure reason internally but never expose it in the response

title: — Remove Hardcoded JWT Secret Fallback (auth-service, identity-service)
— FIDO2 / WebAuthn Assertion Properly Verified (identity-service)
content:
File: services/identity-service/src/index.js

The /auth/fido2/authenticate/verify endpoint issues a full JWT after only
checking that the credential ID exists. It never verifies the assertion
signature or the authenticator counter.

Implementation:
- Install fido2-lib
- On verify, call f2l.assertionResult(assertionResponse, { challenge, origin, factor: "either" })
- Reject if verification throws or returns falsy
- After successful verification read authnrData.counter
- Query the stored counter for this credential from the database
- If new counter is less than or equal to the stored counter, reject with 401
  and log a replay attack warning
- If valid, update the stored counter to the new value before issuing JWT

title: — ECDSA Signature Verification in Smart Contract (AegisTradeEscrow.sol)
content:
File: smart-contracts/contracts/AegisTradeEscrow.sol

The fulfillCondition() function accepts a documentSignature parameter but
never validates it. Any oracle with ORACLE_ROLE can fulfill conditions with
arbitrary data.

Implementation:
- Import OpenZeppelin ECDSA library
- Add a logisticsProvider address field to the Escrow struct
- Set logisticsProvider when the escrow is created
- In fulfillCondition(), recover the signer address:

    address recovered = ECDSA.recover(
      keccak256(abi.encodePacked(escrowId, conditionIndex, documentHash)),
      documentSignature
    );
    require(recovered == escrow.logisticsProvider, "Invalid logistics provider signature");

- Emit a log event recording the recovered address and document hash

title: — Wire isMultiSig into releaseFunds() (AegisTradeEscrow.sol)
content:
File: smart-contracts/contracts/AegisTradeEscrow.sol

The isMultiSig flag on the Escrow struct is set but never read. Multi-sig
escrows release identically to regular ones.

Implementation:
- Add a signRelease(uint256 escrowId) function
- Track signatures in the existing escrowSignatures mapping
- Track how many unique authorized signers have signed per escrow
- In releaseFunds() add:

    if (e.isMultiSig) {
      require(escrowSignatures[escrowId].count >= e.requiredSignatures,
        "Insufficient signatures for multi-sig release");
    }

- Emit a SignatureCollected event each time a valid signer calls signRelease()
- Emit a ThresholdReached event when the required count is met

title: — claimRefund Logic Bug (AegisTradeEscrow.sol)
content:
File: smart-contracts/contracts/AegisTradeEscrow.sol

claimRefund() currently allows a refund when status is CONDITIONS_MET.
A malicious buyer can claim a refund even after the seller has delivered
all trade documents.

Implementation:
Load the escrow struct first, then apply these exact checks:

    Escrow storage e = escrows[escrowId];
    require(e.status == EscrowStatus.FUNDED, "Refund only allowed before conditions met");
    require(block.timestamp > e.expiryTimestamp, "Escrow not yet expired");

Note: use e.status and e.expiryTimestamp — those are the actual storage
variable names. Do not use bare status or escrow.expiryTime.
Add a Solidity unit test that calls fulfillCondition() then asserts
claimRefund() reverts.

title: — MFA TOTP Secret Encrypted at Application Layer (identity-service)
— Director and UBO PII Encrypted at Application Layer (kyb-service)
content:
File: services/kyb-service/src/index.js

The directors and ubos JSONB columns are inserted and read as plain JSON
objects. The comment says "Encrypted PII in real deployment" but no
encryption code exists.

Implementation:
- Reuse the same encrypt() and decrypt() functions from Fix 23
- Before INSERT: JSON.stringify(directors), then encrypt the string
- After SELECT: decrypt the blob, then JSON.parse()
- Apply to both directors and ubos columns in every INSERT and SELECT
- Write a migration script to encrypt all existing plaintext rows
- Note for the engineering team in a comment: raw PII has been persisted.
  Depending on jurisdiction a breach notification assessment may be required.
  Flag this to the Data Protection Officer.

title: — M2M Tokens Use Separate Signing Key from User JWTs (auth-service)
content:
File: services/auth-service/src/index.js

The /auth/token machine-to-machine endpoint signs service account tokens
with the same JWT_SECRET used for human user sessions. A single key
compromise exposes both simultaneously.

Implementation:
- Add JWT_M2M_SECRET as a required environment variable with the same
  crash-fast startup check as JWT_SECRET
- Sign M2M tokens with JWT_M2M_SECRET and include keyid: 'm2m-v1' in the
  header options
- Sign user tokens with JWT_SECRET and include keyid: 'user-v1' in the
  header options
- In requireAuth() use the kid header to select the verification key:

    const decoded = jwt.decode(token, { complete: true });
    const kid = decoded?.header?.kid;
    const key = kid === 'm2m-v1' ? process.env.JWT_M2M_SECRET : process.env.JWT_SECRET;
    const algo = kid === 'm2m-v1' ? 'HS256' : 'RS256';
    jwt.verify(token, key, { algorithms: [algo] });

  A forged kid header simply fails verification with the wrong key.
  No privilege escalation is possible from a kid mismatch.

title: — RS256 Requires RSA PEM, Not a Random String (identity-service)
content:
File: services/identity-service/src/index.js and .env.example

signToken() uses algorithm: "RS256" but .env.example sets
JWT_SECRET=CHANGE_ME_64_CHAR_RANDOM — a symmetric string. The service
crashes with a PEM parse error on every real login attempt.

Implementation:
- Rename JWT_SECRET to JWT_PRIVATE_KEY throughout identity-service
- Rename references to JWT_PUBLIC_KEY in wallet-service and websocket-service
  (they already expect a public key — just make the naming consistent)
- Update .env.example:

    JWT_PRIVATE_KEY=<paste RSA private key PEM here, newlines as \n>
    JWT_PUBLIC_KEY=<paste RSA public key PEM here, newlines as \n>

- Add generation instructions in .env.example comments:

    # Generate with:
    # openssl genrsa -out private.pem 2048
    # openssl rsa -in private.pem -pubout -out public.pem

- The MFA temporary token (which uses no algorithm option and defaults to
  HS256) should be explicitly signed with a separate JWT_TEMP_SECRET to
  make the intent clear. Add JWT_TEMP_SECRET to .env.example as required.

title: — WebSocket JWT Not Passed in URL Query String (websocket-service)
— Prompt Injection in AI Service (ai-service)
content:
File: services/ai-service/src/index.js

Raw req.body fields including transaction, ocrText, and extractedData are
interpolated directly into Claude prompts. Any user can inject instructions
to manipulate AML risk scores.

Implementation:
- Install zod
- Define a strict schema for each endpoint's input:

    const transactionSchema = z.object({
      amount: z.number().positive(),
      currency: z.enum(['USDC', 'USDT', 'AE_COIN', 'USDC_SOL']),
      senderCountry: z.string().length(2),
      receiverCountry: z.string().length(2),
      transactionType: z.enum(['transfer', 'escrow', 'fiat_ramp'])
    });

- Parse with schema.parse(req.body.transaction) — this throws on unknown
  fields, extra keys, or type mismatches
- Build prompts from the safe parsed object only — never from raw user input
- For OCR endpoints, extract only named typed fields from the OCR result
  before passing to the prompt. Never pass raw ocrText strings.
- Return 400 if the input fails schema validation

title: — SSRF Protection on Webhook Registration (webhook-service)
— Deployer Private Key Removed from Application Environment
content:
File: .env.example and smart-contracts/scripts/deploy.js

DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY appears in .env.example alongside runtime application
secrets. Any compromised service can read the key controlling all four smart
contracts.

Implementation:
- Remove DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY from .env.example entirely
- Add a comment in its place:

    # DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY must never appear here.
    # Smart contract deployment uses a hardware wallet (Ledger) or a
    # CI/CD-only secrets vault. It is never available to running services.

- In deploy.js, read the key from process.env.DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY but add:

    if (!process.env.DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY) {
      throw new Error('DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY must be injected by the CI/CD pipeline only. Never store it in .env.');
    }

- Add a pre-commit git hook:

    #!/bin/sh
    if grep -r "DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY\s*=" .env* 2>/dev/null; then
      echo "ERROR: DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY found in env files. Remove it immediately."
      exit 1
    fi

- Add the same check to CI pipeline as a required status check

title: — Oracle Private Key Moved Out of Application Runtime (trade-service)
content:
File: services/trade-service/src/index.js

ORACLE_PRIVATE_KEY lives in the same runtime environment as all other
service secrets. It can call fulfillCondition() on any escrow — essentially
forging trade document delivery.

Implementation:
- If AWS KMS is available, use a KMS-backed signer:

    const { KMSSigner } = require('@openzeppelin/defender-sdk');
    const signer = new KMSSigner({ keyId: process.env.ORACLE_KMS_KEY_ID });

- If KMS is not immediately available, extract all oracle signing into a
  dedicated oracle-service with:
  - No external network access (no egress except to the blockchain RPC)
  - Its own Docker network segment isolated from wallet-service and identity-service
  - Its own Kubernetes ServiceAccount with a separate secrets scope
  - No shared environment variables with any other service
- Remove ORACLE_PRIVATE_KEY from the main .env.example
- Add ORACLE_KMS_KEY_ID as its replacement

title: — Password Reset Tokens Not Published to Kafka (extensions.js)
content:
File: services/identity-service/src/modules/extensions.js

publishEvent("auth.password_reset", { email, resetToken: token }) sends
the raw usable reset token over Kafka. Kafka has no authentication (separate
issue) so any process on the network can harvest every token issued.

Implementation:
- Never publish sensitive credentials to any event bus
- Instead publish only a non-sensitive notification event:

    publishEvent('auth.password_reset_requested', {
      email,
      requestedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
      ipAddress: req.ip
    });

- The reset token itself is only sent directly to the user's email via the
  notification service
- If downstream consumers need to know a reset happened for audit purposes,
  publish the hashed token using SHA-256 — never the raw token

title: — Backup Codes Not Published to Kafka (extensions.js)
content:
File: services/identity-service/src/modules/extensions.js

publishEvent("auth.backup_codes_generated", { email, codes: formatted })
sends all ten plaintext backup codes over Kafka. Backup codes permanently
bypass TOTP.

Implementation:
- Remove the codes field from this event payload entirely
- Publish only:

    publishEvent('auth.backup_codes_regenerated', {
      email,
      regeneratedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
      codeCount: 10
    });

- The codes themselves are returned only in the HTTP response to the
  authenticated user who requested regeneration, never to the event bus

title: — Hardcoded Demo API Key in Business Model Service (business-model)
— Hardcoded Credentials in Load Test Files (load-tests)
content:
Files: infrastructure/load-tests/k6-load-test.js, infrastructure/load-tests/main.js

Both files contain hardcoded email addresses and passwords used for load
testing. If these accounts exist in staging or production they are
permanent backdoors readable to anyone with repo access.

Implementation:
- Replace all hardcoded credentials with environment variable reads:

    const TEST_EMAIL = __ENV.LOAD_TEST_EMAIL;
    const TEST_PASSWORD = __ENV.LOAD_TEST_PASSWORD;

  (k6 reads from __ENV, not process.env)
- For main.js: const TEST_PASSWORD = process.env.LOAD_TEST_PASSWORD;
- Delete the test accounts from staging and production databases
- Document in the load test README that these variables must be set before
  running and must point to isolated test-environment accounts that do not
  exist in production
- Add to .gitignore: any .env file that would hold load test credentials

---

title: — Each Service Gets Its Own Database Schema
— Redis Password Enforced
content:
File: infrastructure/docker-compose.yml

Redis runs with no password. Any process on the Docker network can read
all session tokens, rate limit counters, FIDO2 challenges, and revocation lists.

Implementation:
- Add to the redis service command:

    command: redis-server --appendonly yes --requirepass ${REDIS_PASSWORD}

- Add REDIS_PASSWORD to .env.example as a required entry
- Update all service Redis connection strings to use the password:
  redis://:${REDIS_PASSWORD}@redis:6379

title: — Kafka Cluster With 3 Brokers and Replication Factor 3
content:
File: infrastructure/docker-compose.yml

A single Kafka broker and single ZooKeeper instance means a single point
of failure on the event backbone of a payment platform.

Implementation:
- Add kafka-2 and kafka-3 broker definitions to docker-compose.yml following
  the same pattern as the existing kafka service but with distinct
  KAFKA_BROKER_ID values (2 and 3)
- Set on all brokers:
  KAFKA_DEFAULT_REPLICATION_FACTOR=3
  KAFKA_MIN_INSYNC_REPLICAS=2
  KAFKA_OFFSETS_TOPIC_REPLICATION_FACTOR=3
- For production Kubernetes, add a note recommending the Strimzi Kafka
  operator with a KafkaNodePool of 3 replicas
- Set replication.factor=3 explicitly when creating all payment-critical topics

title: — Multi-Region Disaster Recovery
content:
File: infrastructure/k8s/deployment.yaml, infrastructure/scripts/backup.sh

Everything runs in a single AWS region (me-central-1). A regional outage
takes the entire platform offline.

Implementation:
- Add instructions and Terraform/CDK snippets to enable:
  - RDS Multi-AZ for PostgreSQL (synchronous standby in a second AZ)
  - An RDS Read Replica in eu-west-1 as a warm standby
  - S3 Cross-Region Replication on the backup bucket to eu-west-1
  - Route 53 health check with failover routing record pointing to the
    primary EKS cluster in me-central-1 with failover to eu-west-1
- Add runbook documentation: RTO target 4 hours, RPO target 1 hour
- Add a quarterly failover drill reminder comment in the backup script

title: — All Service Ports Removed from Host, Proper Network Isolation
content:
File: infrastructure/docker-compose.yml

Every service maps its container port to localhost:30XX. The wallet service,
compliance engine, and identity service are all reachable directly,
bypassing NGINX entirely.

Implementation:
- Remove every ports: mapping from all service definitions except NGINX
- Replace with expose: for documentation (note: expose: has no enforcement
  effect — the security comes entirely from removing ports:)
- Define two named networks:

    networks:
      frontend-net:
      backend-net:

- Attach only NGINX to both networks:

    nginx:
      networks:
        - frontend-net
        - backend-net

- Attach all other services only to backend-net:

    identity-service:
      networks:
        - backend-net

- This prevents frontend-net containers from reaching backend services directly

title: — Escrow ID Uses block.number and Nonce (AegisTradeEscrow.sol)
— TLS Configured on NGINX, Port 443 Active
— All 18 Services Routed Through NGINX
— Distributed Lock on All Scheduler Cron Jobs (scheduler-service)
— trading-service Recurring Payment Cron Removed Entirely
— Backup Rotation Moved to S3 Lifecycle Rules
content:
File: infrastructure/scripts/backup.sh

The backup rotation uses date -d with GNU-specific syntax that fails on
Alpine Linux and macOS. Old backups are never deleted if the regex fails.

Implementation:
- Remove the date-based rotation logic from backup.sh entirely
- Add an S3 Lifecycle Rule in the infrastructure config (Terraform or CDK):

    Lifecycle rule: expire objects with prefix "backups/" after 30 days
    Transition to Glacier after 7 days for cost optimization

- If Terraform is not available, add the aws CLI command to the backup
  script's README:

    aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration \
      --bucket ${BACKUP_BUCKET} \
      --lifecycle-configuration file://lifecycle.json

---

title: — Russia Reclassified HIGH_RISK, EDD Mandatory
content:
File: services/compliance-engine/src/index.js,
      services/compliance-service/src/main.py

Russia is currently classified as MED_RISK. Regulators and correspondent
banks treat Russia-origin flows as high-risk without exception.

Implementation:
- Change Russia's risk classification from MED_RISK to HIGH_RISK in the
  country risk table
- Add a mandatory Enhanced Due Diligence flag for any entity where:
  - Country of incorporation is Russia
  - Any UBO has Russian nationality or Russian address
  - Any beneficial owner holds more than 10% and has Russian connections
- Block auto-approval for all HIGH_RISK entities
- Route them to the Compliance Officer queue with:
  - A mandatory OFAC re-check
  - A mandatory EU consolidated list check
  - A mandatory UK HMT sanctions check
  - A required EDD notes field before approval is possible

title: — PRODUCTION_MODE Gate for Regulatory Compliance
content:
File: services/wallet-service/src/index.js, services/fiat-service/src/index.js,
      and all services handling real fund movement

The platform can process real funds before VARA license approval and
ISO 27001 certification.

Implementation:
- Add a PRODUCTION_MODE environment variable defaulting to false
- In wallet-service, if PRODUCTION_MODE !== 'true':

    if (process.env.PRODUCTION_MODE !== 'true') {
      return res.status(503).json({
        error: 'Platform is operating in restricted mode pending regulatory approval.',
        code: 'REGULATORY_PENDING'
      });
    }

- Apply this gate to: wallet provisioning, real transfers, fiat on/off-ramp,
  and any endpoint that moves real funds
- Document clearly: PRODUCTION_MODE=true must only be set after:
  1. VARA VASP license is granted
  2. ISO 27001 certification is complete
  3. A named smart contract audit firm has completed and published their report

title: — Audit Placeholder Removed, Mainnet Deploy Locked
— On-Chain Arbitration Flow with Deadline
— Travel Rule VASP ID Sourced from Config, Not Hardcoded
content:
File: services/compliance-engine/src/index.js

sendingVasp: "AegisLedger-VARA-2024-0047" is hardcoded into every IVMS101
packet. The VARA license application is still in progress, making this
either a fabricated or provisional identifier.

Implementation:
- Add VARA_VASP_ID as a required environment variable with crash-fast check
- Add a format validator:

    const VASP_ID_FORMAT = /^[A-Za-z0-9\-]+$/;
    if (!VASP_ID_FORMAT.test(process.env.VARA_VASP_ID)) {
      throw new Error('VARA_VASP_ID format is invalid');
    }

- In staging, set VARA_VASP_ID=TEST-AegisLedger-STAGING so counterparty
  VASPs can distinguish test packets from live ones
- Add VARA_VASP_ID to .env.example with a comment explaining the VARA
  registration process and the expected identifier format

---

title: — Zod Validation on All Service Endpoints
— Unit, Integration, and Contract Tests Added
content:
Files: all services, all smart contracts

There are 66 files and zero test files. This makes any refactoring
extremely risky.

Implementation:
- Install Jest and Supertest for all Node.js services
- Install pytest for compliance-service
- Install Hardhat's built-in Mocha and Chai for smart contracts
- Write tests for at minimum these critical paths:
  - auth-service: login, MFA verify, SSO verify, token refresh, logout
  - identity-service: FIDO2 register and authenticate, RBAC enforcement
  - wallet-service: provision wallet, initiate transfer, maker-checker approval
  - compliance-engine: AML rule evaluation, sanctions screening, evalCond
  - AegisTradeEscrow.sol: create, fund, fulfill, release, claimRefund,
    initiateDispute, resolveDispute, claimStaleDispute
  - AegisTrancheEscrow.sol: sequential tranche enforcement, out-of-order rejection
- Add a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) that:
  - Runs all tests on every pull request
  - Blocks merge if any test fails
  - Reports coverage and blocks merge below 70% on critical service paths

title: — All Mock Mode Checks Standardized to === "true"
— Route Handlers Deduplicated Between trade-service and trading-service
content:
Files: services/trade-service/src/index.js,
       services/trading-service/src/index.js

Run this audit first:

    grep -E "router\.(get|post|put|patch|delete)\s*\(['\"]" \
      services/trade-service/src/index.js \
      services/trading-service/src/index.js | sort

For every route path that appears in both files:
- Decide the canonical owner:
  - trade-service owns: escrow lifecycle (create, fund, fulfill, dispute, release)
  - trading-service owns: order execution, matching, settlement confirmation
- Delete the handler from the non-owner service
- Update NGINX location blocks to route accordingly:
  /api/v1/escrow/* -> trade-service
  /api/v1/orders/* -> trading-service
- Add route ownership to SERVICES.md
- Add a CI lint step that fails if the same route path appears in both files

title: — AML evalCond Whitelist and Transaction Sanitization (compliance-engine)
— API Versioned to /api/v1/ With Correct NGINX Rewrite
content:
File: infrastructure/nginx/gateway.conf, all service router definitions

No version prefix exists on any endpoint. Breaking changes will break all
clients simultaneously.

Implementation:
- In all service files, change: app.use('/api', router)
  to: app.use('/api/v1', router)
- In NGINX, add a rewrite for legacy /api/ requests:

    rewrite ^/api/(.*)$ /api/v1/$1 permanent;

  Note: use $1 capture group — never $request_uri after a prefix,
  as $request_uri includes the full original path and produces double-path
  URLs like /api/v1//api/wallets/...
- Add a deprecation policy comment: v1 is frozen once institutional clients
  are live. Breaking changes go into v2 with a 6-month deprecation notice.
- Add an API-Version: 1 response header to every endpoint

title: — Wallet Service Kafka Publisher Fully Implemented (wallet-service)
— AegisBoLNFT Rewritten With OpenZeppelin ERC1155
— AegisMultiSig Ownership Recovery With Ownable2Step
— AegisTrancheEscrow Enforces Sequential Tranche Order
— IDOR on Dashboard Config Endpoint (analytics-service or developer-portal)
content:
The GET /dashboard-config/:userId endpoint uses req.params.userId instead
of the authenticated user's ID from their JWT.

Implementation:
- Remove :userId from the route entirely: GET /dashboard-config
- Replace the query parameter:

    const config = await db.query(
      'SELECT * FROM dashboard_config WHERE user_id = $1',
      [req.user.id]
    );

- Audit every endpoint that accepts a userId or companyId in the URL.
  For each one, verify that the value is compared against req.user.id
  or req.user.companyId from the verified token — never trusted from params.

title: — Analytics Endpoints Enforce Company Isolation (analytics-service)
content:
The /analytics/latency endpoint queries all transfers with no company filter.
The /analytics/fee-revenue endpoint returns financial data for all companies.

Implementation:
- Add WHERE company_id = $1 to every analytics query:

    SELECT
      AVG(settlement_time_ms) AS avg_latency,
      percentile_cont(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY settlement_time_ms) AS p95_latency
    FROM transfers
    WHERE company_id = $1
      AND created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
    -- $1 = req.user.companyId from verified JWT

- The topCompanies leaderboard must be restricted to ADMIN role only.
  Add a role check before that query:

    if (req.user.role !== 'ADMIN') {
      return res.status(403).json({ error: 'Insufficient permissions' });
    }

title: — OpenPayd Webhook HMAC Verification (fiat-service)
— Stripe Webhook Handler Added (billing-service)
— BoL Transfer Ownership Check (trade-service)
— Letter of Credit Draw Ownership Check (trade-service)
— AE Coin Exchange Rate From Live Oracle (wallet-service or billing-service)
— FX Rates From Live Source in business-model Service
— Depeg Detection and FX Quote Expiry
— KYB Document Uploads Streamed to S3, Not Stored in RAM
— infra-ops CLI Arguments Use spawnSync With Argument Array
— Treasury Multi-Sig Address as Fee Recipient in deploy.js
— Feature Flag System for Phase 2 and 3
— AE Coin Liquidity Check With USDC Fallback
